skill.name: pubchem-database
skill.description: Query PubChem with PubChemPy and PUG-REST to search compounds, retrieve molecular properties, run structure searches, and inspect bioactivity data.
skill.summary: Search PubChem compounds, properties, structures, and bioactivity data from AI coding tools.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, and chemistry terms such as CID, InChI, and SMILES. The confirmed risks are intentional outbound requests to PubChem and optional local file writes for downloaded compound structures, so publication is reasonable with a clear network and filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: Intentional External PubChem API Requests
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The helper script builds PubChem PUG-REST and PUG-View URLs and sends GET or POST requests through requests. This is expected for a PubChem research skill, but user queries and compound identifiers leave the local environment for NCBI PubChem. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The URLs and request calls are explicit, limited to PubChem endpoints, and match the documented purpose of the skill.

medium_findings.title: Local Structure Downloads Can Overwrite Files
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The compound download helper accepts a filename and calls pcp.download with overwrite enabled, and the documentation shows writing structure image content to disk. This is legitimate export behavior, but users should choose output paths carefully because an existing file could be replaced. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The write behavior is directly visible and intentional, but it is user-directed rather than covert or malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections are Markdown code fences, inline endpoint notation, and example snippets, not Ruby backtick shell execution. No evidence found of subprocess, os.system, eval, or shell command construction in the reviewed files. Confidence: 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines are documentation formatting or Python examples, and the actual scripts do not invoke a shell.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Chemistry Terminology
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The high severity cryptography matches refer to chemistry identifiers and property names such as CID, InChI, SMILES, and assay IDs. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, custom encryption, credential handling, or cryptographic verification. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The surrounding context is chemical data retrieval and molecular representation, not security-sensitive cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Scan Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt injection concern. Reviewed skill files did not contain override commands, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security review. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: Targeted search across the files from the static analysis found no suspicious prompt-injection phrases, but absence of evidence cannot prove all future content safe.

dangerous_patterns.title: Intentional External PubChem API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The helper script builds PubChem PUG-REST and PUG-View URLs and sends GET or POST requests through requests. This is expected for a PubChem research skill, but user queries and compound identifiers leave the local environment for NCBI PubChem. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The URLs and request calls are explicit, limited to PubChem endpoints, and match the documented purpose of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Structure Downloads Can Overwrite Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The compound download helper accepts a filename and calls pcp.download with overwrite enabled, and the documentation shows writing structure image content to disk. This is legitimate export behavior, but users should choose output paths carefully because an existing file could be replaced. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The write behavior is directly visible and intentional, but it is user-directed rather than covert or malicious.

content.value_statement: Chemical research often requires fast access to identifiers, molecular properties, structure matches, and assay data. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PubChemPy and PUG-REST workflows for repeatable compound lookup and analysis.
actual_capabilities: Search PubChem compounds by name, CID, SMILES, InChI, or molecular formula.
Retrieve molecular formula, weight, IUPAC name, SMILES, InChI, XLogP, TPSA, and hydrogen bond counts.
Run similarity and substructure searches with configurable thresholds and result limits.
Convert compound identifiers and download structures as SDF, JSON, or PNG files.
Query PubChem bioassay summaries, assay descriptions, targets, active compounds, and PUG-View annotations.
Batch process compound lists for property comparison and drug-likeness screening examples.
limitations: Requires network access to PubChem or PubChemPy for live data retrieval.
Large similarity and substructure searches can take 15 to 30 seconds or time out.
Returned data depends on PubChem coverage and may omit some properties or annotations.
The skill supports research workflows and does not validate clinical or regulatory conclusions.

use_cases.title: Compound Property Lookup
use_cases.description: Find a compound by name or CID and summarize key molecular descriptors for early research review.

use_cases.title: Structure-Based Candidate Search
use_cases.description: Use SMILES input to find similar compounds or structures containing a selected chemical motif.

use_cases.title: Bioactivity Triage
use_cases.description: Retrieve assay summaries and active compound lists for a target or compound before deeper analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Look Up One Compound
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PubChem skill to look up aspirin. Return its CID, formula, molecular weight, canonical SMILES, InChIKey, XLogP, and TPSA.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Compound Properties
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PubChem skill to compare aspirin, ibuprofen, and naproxen. Create a concise table of formula, molecular weight, XLogP, TPSA, hydrogen bond donors, and hydrogen bond acceptors.

prompt_templates.title: Find Similar Structures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PubChem skill to search for compounds similar to this SMILES string: c1ccccc1. Use a 90 percent similarity threshold and summarize the top 10 results.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Target Bioactivity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PubChem bioactivity helper approach to find assays for EGFR, list active compound CIDs from the first relevant assays, and summarize any caveats about assay interpretation.

output_examples.output: A concise compound summary with CID, molecular formula, molecular weight, canonical SMILES, IUPAC name, XLogP, TPSA, and common synonyms.

output_examples.input: Compare aspirin, ibuprofen, and paracetamol for drug-like properties.
output_examples.output: A readable comparison table with molecular descriptors, Lipinski-style rule checks, missing-value notes, and a short interpretation.

output_examples.input: Find active compounds for EGFR assays.
output_examples.output: A filtered list of assay IDs and active compound CIDs, with a summary of result limits and PubChem assay coverage caveats.

best_practices: Use CIDs for repeated queries because they are more precise than names or synonyms.
Set MaxRecords and delays for broad searches to reduce timeouts and respect PubChem rate limits.
Cache or save only needed outputs, and choose explicit filenames for downloaded structures.
anti_patterns: Do not treat PubChem bioactivity hits as validated clinical evidence without expert review.
Do not run unbounded similarity or substructure searches without result limits.
Do not overwrite important local files when downloading SDF, JSON, or PNG structure outputs.

faq.question: What data source does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses PubChem through PubChemPy and the PubChem PUG-REST and PUG-View APIs.

faq.question: Can it search by SMILES or InChI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents searches by name, CID, SMILES, InChI, and molecular formula.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. Live compound, structure, and assay queries require outbound access to PubChem services.

faq.question: Can it download chemical structures?
faq.answer: Yes. It can download structures in formats such as SDF, JSON, and PNG when a filename is provided.

faq.question: Does it handle rate limits?
faq.answer: The bioactivity helper includes a short request delay, and the documentation recommends limiting records and caching results.

faq.question: Is it suitable for medical decisions?
faq.answer: No. It supports research data retrieval and requires expert validation for clinical, safety, or regulatory use.
